subsystem: GObject/GTK class initialization for GSWindow, the full-screen lock/blanking window of gnome-screensaver. gs_window_class_init installs the GObjectClass finalize/get/set-property slots, overrides a set of GtkWidget event vtable handlers (key press, pointer motion, button, scroll, grab-broken, visibility-notify, popup-menu), registers two signals (activity, deactivated), and declares the object's GObject properties (obscured, dialog-up, lock-enabled, logout-enabled, logout-timeout, logout-command, keyboard-enabled, keyboard-command, monitor).
provenance: Identified from the GObject/GTK idioms and symbol names: G_OBJECT_CLASS/GTK_WIDGET_CLASS casts, g_type_class_add_private, g_signal_new, g_object_class_install_property, and g_param_spec_*. The type name GSWindow/GSWindowClass/GSWindowPrivate, the gs_window_* handler prefix, and properties like lock-enabled, logout-command, keyboard-command and the Xinerama 'monitor' property identify this as the gs-window.c class_init of gnome-screensaver (GS = GNOME Screensaver). The header banner labels the sample corpus/train/train_0035_cwe284.c and the leading comment 'primevul_c_train_2789_fixed' marks it a PrimeVul-derived CWE-284 (Improper Access Control) sample.
trust_boundary: This is registration/declaration code that runs once at type initialization with no attacker-controlled input at this site. The trust boundary it DEFINES is the security-critical one, however: GSWindow is the screen-lock window shown to an attacker with physical console access. The GtkWidget event handlers wired here (key_press_event, motion_notify_event, button_press_event, scroll_event, grab_broken_event, visibility_notify_event, popup_menu) are the entire input attack surface a person at a locked machine can reach via keyboard, mouse and the X server, and the grab-broken / visibility events are attacker-influenceable via other X clients. The string properties logout-command and keyboard-command carry command lines that the implementation later spawns; their trust depends on whether they originate from privileged config (GSettings/GConf) or from a lower-trust source. All the real logic lives in the gs_window_real_* callbacks (implementation not provided).

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the GObject class_init function for the GSWindow type. It receives the freshly allocated GSWindowClass vtable and casts it to the two base-class vtables it derives from: GObjectClass (the root object system, providing construction/finalization and the property machinery) and GtkWidgetClass (the GTK widget base, providing the event-handler function pointers). All subsequent assignments write into these two vtables to override the parent behavior for every instance of GSWindow. Because a class_init runs exactly once per type at first use, everything installed here becomes global policy for the lock window.

Installs the three core GObjectClass slots. finalize (gs_window_finalize) is the destructor that releases the instance's private data and any grabbed resources when the object's refcount reaches zero. get_property/set_property (gs_window_get_property/gs_window_set_property) are the dispatchers GObject calls whenever code reads or writes one of the properties declared later; they translate a property id into a field of GSWindowPrivate. These are the only paths through which the lock-enabled, logout-command, keyboard-command, monitor, etc. values are actually applied to a live window.

Overrides the GtkWidget lifecycle vtable entries. show/hide (gs_window_real_show/gs_window_real_hide) control when the lock window is mapped on screen and are the natural place to establish or drop the keyboard/pointer grab and to raise the window above everything else. realize/unrealize (gs_window_real_realize/gs_window_real_unrealize) run when the underlying GdkWindow (the X resource) is created or destroyed and typically set X window attributes such as override-redirect, input focus, and event masks. For a lock screen these lifecycle hooks are where the 'nobody can interact with the session behind me' guarantee is set up and torn down.

Overrides the four raw input event handlers. key_press_event (gs_window_real_key_press_event) receives every keystroke while the lock window holds the keyboard grab; it must decide which keys wake the screensaver / feed the unlock dialog and which are swallowed, and it is the single most security-relevant handler because a keystroke that leaks through can reach the session behind the lock. motion_notify_event, button_press_event and scroll_event (gs_window_real_*) receive pointer movement, clicks and scroll wheel input; on a lock screen these normally just signal 'user activity' to wake the fade/dialog, and must not forward the event to any window underneath.

get_preferred_width/get_preferred_height (gs_window_real_get_preferred_*) report the widget's size-request to GTK's geometry code; for a full-screen locker these are cosmetic. The remaining three are core to the lock guarantee: grab_broken_event (gs_window_real_grab_broken) fires when the X server revokes the keyboard or pointer grab this window holds (another client grabbed, a VT switch, etc.) and is where the code must re-acquire the grab or otherwise refuse to leave the session exposed. visibility_notify_event (gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event) fires when the window becomes partially or fully obscured and is where a locker re-raises itself so no other window can sit on top and receive input. popup_menu (gs_window_real_popup_menu) handles the keyboard 'menu' key / context-menu request; overriding it lets the locker suppress any context menu that might otherwise offer an escape from the locked state.

Registers the size of the per-instance private structure GSWindowPrivate with the type system so that each GSWindow instance gets an opaque, hidden block of that size accessible via the generated get_private accessor. This is where the runtime state of the lock window (the grab state, the current logout/keyboard command strings, the monitor index, the enabled flags, etc.) is stored. Nothing here is attacker-facing, but every property setter writes into this block.

what_the_code_does.code:
signals [ACTIVITY] =
                g_signal_new ("activity",
                              G_TYPE_FROM_CLASS (object_class),
                              G_SIGNAL_RUN_LAST,
                              G_STRUCT_OFFSET (GSWindowClass, activity),
                              NULL,
                              NULL,
                              gs_marshal_BOOLEAN__VOID,
                              G_TYPE_BOOLEAN,
                              0);
        signals [DEACTIVATED] =
                g_signal_new ("deactivated",
                              G_TYPE_FROM_CLASS (object_class),
                              G_SIGNAL_RUN_LAST,
                              G_STRUCT_OFFSET (GSWindowClass, deactivated),
                              NULL,
                              NULL,
                              g_cclosure_marshal_VOID__VOID,
                              G_TYPE_NONE,
                              0);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Registers the two GObject signals the window emits. 'activity' (signals[ACTIVITY]) returns a gboolean (marshaller gs_marshal_BOOLEAN__VOID, return type G_TYPE_BOOLEAN) and is emitted when the window detects user activity that should wake the saver; the boolean return lets a handler veto/consume the activity. 'deactivated' (signals[DEACTIVATED]) returns void and is emitted when the window is dismissed/unlocked. Both use G_SIGNAL_RUN_LAST (the class default handler runs after connected handlers) and store the class default-handler slot offset via G_STRUCT_OFFSET into GSWindowClass. The '0' final argument means neither signal carries any parameters. These are the notification channel by which the manager decides whether to unlock the session, so 'deactivated' is trust-sensitive: whoever can cause it to fire causes the lock to drop.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_OBSCURED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("obscured",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READABLE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_DIALOG_UP,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("dialog-up",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READABLE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOCK_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("lock-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOGOUT_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("logout-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares four boolean properties. 'obscured' (PROP_OBSCURED) and 'dialog-up' (PROP_DIALOG_UP) are read-only (G_PARAM_READABLE) status flags reflecting whether the window is currently obscured and whether the unlock dialog is showing. 'lock-enabled' (PROP_LOCK_ENABLED) is the master switch that decides whether dismissing the saver requires authentication or simply unblanks; it is read-write, so whatever component can set this property controls whether the machine actually locks — a security-defining flag. 'logout-enabled' (PROP_LOGOUT_ENABLED) toggles whether the idle-logout affordance is offered. All default to FALSE. The empty NULL nick/blurb arguments mean these are internal, not user-visible, property descriptions.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOGOUT_TIMEOUT,
                                         g_param_spec_long ("logout-timeout",
                                                            NULL,
                                                            NULL,
                                                            -1,
                                                            G_MAXLONG,
                                                            0,
                                                            G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_LOGOUT_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("logout-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the logout controls. 'logout-timeout' (PROP_LOGOUT_TIMEOUT) is a glong with range -1..G_MAXLONG default 0, the delay before the idle-logout button appears. 'logout-command' (PROP_LOGOUT_COMMAND) is a read-write string (default NULL) that holds the shell command line the window will spawn when the user triggers logout. This string property is a code-execution surface: the value flows into gs_window_set_property and is later handed to a spawn routine, so its provenance and how it is tokenized/escaped decide whether it is safe.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("keyboard-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
        g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("keyboard-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the on-screen-keyboard controls. 'keyboard-enabled' (PROP_KEYBOARD_ENABLED) is a read-write boolean (default FALSE) gating whether an embedded on-screen keyboard is shown on the lock screen. 'keyboard-command' (PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND) is a read-write string (default NULL) holding the command line for the OSK helper (historically something like gok/onboard) that the locker spawns and embeds via XEMBED. Like logout-command, this string is later executed, so it is a second command-execution surface declared here.

what_the_code_does.code:
g_object_class_install_property (object_class,
                                         PROP_MONITOR,
                                         g_param_spec_int ("monitor",
                                                           "Xinerama monitor",
                                                           "The monitor (in terms of Xinerama) which the window is on",
                                                           0,
                                                           G_MAXINT,
                                                           0,
                                                           G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT));

}
what_the_code_does.explanation: Declares the last property and closes the function. 'monitor' (PROP_MONITOR) is a gint in the range 0..G_MAXINT, default 0, carrying the Xinerama/RandR monitor index the lock window covers. Unlike the others it adds G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT, meaning GObject will set it (to the default 0 if the caller supplies nothing) during construction so the window always has a monitor assigned. The value is later used to index monitor geometry arrays. The closing brace ends gs_window_class_init; there is no cleanup or error handling because none of these GObject registration calls can fail recoverably.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This handler is the linchpin of the lock guarantee and the class of bug this CWE-284 sample is about (screen-lock bypass, improper access control). A GSWindow is only a lock as long as it holds the X keyboard and pointer grab; grab_broken_event fires precisely when that grab is lost — for example when another X client calls XGrabKeyboard, when a menu/DnD grab elsewhere pre-empts it, or across a VT switch. The invariant the implementation gs_window_real_grab_broken MUST uphold is: on any grab-broken, either re-establish the grab (and keep re-trying) or drive the window into a fail-closed state, never quietly continue with no grab. If the handler mishandles the event (ignores it, returns without re-grabbing, or is confused about which of keyboard/pointer broke), keystrokes and clicks flow to whatever window is behind the locker and the attacker types straight into the unlocked session. This is a latent contract: the bug, if present, lives in gs_window_real_grab_broken which is not shown here. CWE-284 / lock bypass.

key_press_event is the primary attacker input path at a locked console. The implementation gs_window_real_key_press_event must treat every keystroke as hostile: it has to route ordinary characters to the authentication dialog while refusing to let any key perform an action that reaches the session behind the lock (window-manager accelerators, help/URL launchers, mnemonic activation, embedded-widget shortcuts). The historic gnome-screensaver bypass class is exactly this — a keystroke (or a rapid key/dialog interaction) that either dismisses the window without authenticating or triggers an embedded component that spawns a process. The invariant: no code path from a key press may unlock, spawn, or focus-out without a successful authentication. A defect here is latent in the unshown gs_window_real_key_press_event. CWE-284.

Overriding popup_menu is a hardening measure: the default GTK behavior would let the Menu key / Shift-F10 raise a context menu on a focused child widget, and any actionable menu item (open link, save, inspect) is a potential escape from the locked state. The invariant the override must uphold is to suppress the menu entirely (return TRUE without showing anything) on the lock window. If gs_window_real_popup_menu instead falls through to a real menu, or only suppresses it on some children (e.g. not on the embedded keyboard or a label with a URL), it re-opens an interaction channel behind the lock. Latent in the unshown handler. CWE-284.

visibility_notify_event lets the locker notice when another X client has placed a window over it. A cooperating or hostile client can map an override-redirect window on top of the lock surface; if the locker does not react by re-raising itself (and re-asserting the grab / input focus), the overlaid window can receive clicks and keystrokes, or simply hide the fact that the machine is locked. The invariant: on any transition to VISIBILITY_PARTIALLY_OBSCURED/FULLY_OBSCURED the window must raise itself back to the top and keep the grab. A weak or missing reaction in gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event is an access-control weakness (obscured lock). The read-only 'obscured' property (PROP_OBSCURED, line 49) is the state this handler maintains. Latent. CWE-284.

logout-command is a free-form string property that the implementation later spawns as a process when the idle-logout action fires. If the string is parsed with a shell (system()/g_spawn_command_line_*) rather than tokenized and exec'd directly, embedded shell metacharacters in the configured value are interpreted, and if the value can be influenced by a lower-trust source (a per-user GConf/GSettings key read while the screen is locked, or a writable config path) this is an OS command-injection / privilege-context concern. Even without a shell, the command runs in the screensaver's session context. The invariant: the command must come only from trusted (root/policy) configuration and must be executed argv-style without shell interpretation. Latent in gs_window_set_property and the spawn site (not shown). CWE-78.

keyboard-command has the same execution semantics as logout-command: it is spawned to launch the on-screen keyboard helper that is then embedded (XEMBED) inside the lock window while it is displayed. Two distinct hazards: (1) command injection / untrusted-source execution if the string is shell-parsed or attacker-influenced, identical to logout-command; and (2) the embedded child process itself becomes part of the lock UI while unauthenticated — if that helper exposes any way to launch other programs or open a file dialog, it is a bypass surface. The invariant: the command must be trusted policy, executed argv-style, and the embedded helper must be a locked-down component. Latent in the spawn/embed code (not shown). CWE-78 (and contributes to CWE-284).

what_could_go_wrong.code:
PROP_LOCK_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("lock-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: lock-enabled is the boolean that decides whether the window actually enforces authentication or merely blanks. It is G_PARAM_READWRITE and defaults to FALSE. If any code path can flip this to FALSE on a live, displayed window (a stale property write, a race during monitor reconfiguration, a signal handler), the 'deactivated' signal can then drop the window without an auth prompt — a policy bypass. The invariant: lock-enabled must be latched at display time and not be re-evaluated in a way that lets it turn a locked window into a merely-blanked one. This is a design/contract note; the concrete behavior is in gs_window_set_property and the manager (not shown). CWE-284.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
g_param_spec_int ("monitor",
                                                           "Xinerama monitor",
                                                           "The monitor (in terms of Xinerama) which the window is on",
                                                           0,
                                                           G_MAXINT,
                                                           0,
                                                           G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT));
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The monitor index is validated here only as 0..G_MAXINT — an upper bound of G_MAXINT, not the actual monitor count. The value is later used to index the Xinerama/RandR monitor geometry array. If the consuming code trusts the property and indexes without clamping to gdk_screen_get_n_monitors(), an out-of-range value (e.g. a monitor that was hot-unplugged after the property was set, or a stale index) is an out-of-bounds read of the geometry table (CWE-125), producing a mis-sized or mis-placed lock window that may not fully cover a physical screen — again an access-control weakness because an uncovered region exposes the session. The invariant: the implementation must clamp/validate monitor against the live monitor count before using it as an index. Latent in gs_window_set_property / the geometry code (not shown).

summary: This is GObject/GTK class-init boilerplate for gnome-screensaver's lock window; it contains no direct memory-safety defect at this site. Its security weight is entirely in the CONTRACTS it declares: the overridden input/grab/visibility/popup handlers (gs_window_real_*) define the screen-lock bypass surface (CWE-284), and the logout-command / keyboard-command string properties declare command-execution surfaces (CWE-78). Every concrete bug would be latent in the gs_window_real_* callbacks and property setters, which are not included; reviewed blind, the appropriate output is a set of latent contract findings, not a fabricated in-line overflow.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Class-init entry point; runs once at type init with no attacker input — no direct contract.
Destructor slot; standard resource release, no attacker-controlled data at registration.
Property dispatchers; carry the command-string risk covered under logout-command/keyboard-command, no separate contract here.
Lifecycle hooks establishing/tearing down the grab and X attributes; relevant but the enforceable grab contract is captured by grab_broken_event.
Primary keyboard attack path at a locked console; must not let any key bypass authentication.
Pointer/scroll activity handlers; must only signal activity and not forward to windows below — same lock contract, no distinct new failure mode beyond grab handling.
Cosmetic size-request handlers; no security contract.
Central lock guarantee: must re-acquire the grab or fail closed, else session behind lock is exposed.
Must re-raise when obscured; a window placed on top otherwise bypasses/hides the lock.
Override must suppress context menus that could offer an escape from the locked state.
Registers private-struct size; no attacker influence.
Activity notification signal; consumed to wake the saver, not a direct bypass.
Unlock/dismiss notification; the auth gate is enforced before emission, so the contract lives in lock-enabled / key handling, not the signal declaration.
Read-only status flag maintained by visibility_notify_event; not independently writable.
Read-only status flag; no write surface.
Master lock/blank switch; a mid-lifetime flip could drop the window without auth.
Feature toggle for the logout affordance; the execution risk is in logout-command, not this boolean.
Bounded time delay; large values only delay a button, no memory/exec contract.
Free-form command line spawned on logout; command-injection / untrusted-source execution surface.
Toggle for the on-screen keyboard; the exec risk is in keyboard-command.
Command line spawned and embedded while unauthenticated; command-injection and bypass surface.
Upper bound is G_MAXINT not monitor count; used as a geometry-array index — OOB read / uncovered-screen risk if unclamped.

findings.title: Grab-broken handler must re-acquire the input grab or fail closed (lock bypass)
findings.bug_class: Screen-lock bypass via lost input grab (improper access control)
findings.invariant: On every GdkEventGrabBroken (keyboard or pointer), gs_window_real_grab_broken must re-establish the grab it lost (retrying as needed) or transition the window to a fail-closed state; the lock window must never continue displaying while holding no grab.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker (or another X client) causes the window's keyboard/pointer grab to be revoked — e.g. by issuing a competing grab, triggering a menu/DnD grab, or a VT switch. If the handler ignores the event or fails to re-grab, subsequent keystrokes and clicks are delivered to the window behind the locker, giving direct access to the unlocked session.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read gs_window_real_grab_broken: confirm it inspects event->keyboard, always attempts to re-grab (with retry/backoff), and never returns leaving the window shown without a grab.

findings.title: Key-press handler is the primary bypass surface at a locked console
findings.bug_class: Authentication/lock bypass via keystroke handling (improper access control)
findings.invariant: No code path reachable from a key press may dismiss the lock, spawn a process, or transfer focus out of the lock window without a successful authentication; only auth-dialog input and saver-wake are permitted.
findings.failure_mode: A user at the locked screen presses a key (or key combination) that reaches a WM accelerator, a mnemonic, a URL/help launcher, or an embedded child's shortcut, causing the window to unlock, launch a program, or focus a background window — the classic gnome-screensaver keystroke bypass class.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read gs_window_real_key_press_event: confirm keys are consumed/forwarded only to the auth entry, that no accelerator/mnemonic activation escapes, and that any embedded keyboard cannot trigger external actions.

findings.title: logout-command string is spawned — command-injection / untrusted-source execution
findings.code:
PROP_LOGOUT_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("logout-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
findings.bug_class: OS command injection / execution of attacker-influenced command string
findings.invariant: logout-command must originate only from trusted (root/policy) configuration and be executed argv-style (g_spawn with an explicit vector) without shell interpretation of its contents.
findings.failure_mode: If the string is executed via a shell (system()/g_spawn_command_line_*) and its value can be influenced by a lower-trust source (a user-writable GSettings/GConf key or config file read while locked), embedded metacharacters run arbitrary commands in the screensaver's session context.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace where the logout-command GSWindowPrivate field is spawned: confirm argv-style exec (not shell) and that the value comes only from trusted configuration.

findings.title: keyboard-command is spawned and embedded while unauthenticated
findings.code:
PROP_KEYBOARD_COMMAND,
                                         g_param_spec_string ("keyboard-command",
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              NULL,
                                                              G_PARAM_READWRITE));
findings.bug_class: OS command injection plus embedded-helper bypass surface
findings.invariant: keyboard-command must come from trusted policy, be executed argv-style, and the embedded on-screen-keyboard helper must be a locked-down component that cannot launch other programs or open file dialogs while the screen is locked.
findings.failure_mode: A shell-parsed or attacker-influenced keyboard-command runs arbitrary commands; separately, the spawned OSK helper is embedded into the unauthenticated lock UI, so any escape affordance it exposes becomes a lock bypass.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace the keyboard-command spawn/XEMBED path: confirm argv-style exec, trusted source, and that the embedded helper is sandboxed/locked down.

findings.title: popup_menu override must suppress context menus that could offer an escape
findings.bug_class: Lock bypass via context menu (improper access control)
findings.invariant: gs_window_real_popup_menu must unconditionally suppress the context menu (return TRUE without showing one) for the lock window and all of its children.
findings.failure_mode: If the override falls through to a real menu (or fails to cover an embedded child such as the on-screen keyboard or a label containing a URL), an actionable menu item (open link, save, inspect) provides interaction behind the lock.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read gs_window_real_popup_menu: confirm it always suppresses the menu and that no child widget can raise its own context menu.

findings.title: visibility_notify handler must re-raise when the lock window is obscured
findings.bug_class: Lock obscured / bypass via overlaid window (improper access control)
findings.invariant: On any transition to partially/fully obscured, gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event must raise the window back to the top and re-assert grab/focus.
findings.failure_mode: Another X client maps an override-redirect window over the locker; if the handler does not re-raise, the overlay receives input or hides the locked state, exposing or masking the session.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event and the 'obscured' property setter: confirm the window raises itself and keeps the grab on obscure events.

findings.title: monitor property bounded only by G_MAXINT, used as a geometry-array index
findings.code:
g_param_spec_int ("monitor",
                                                           "Xinerama monitor",
                                                           "The monitor (in terms of Xinerama) which the window is on",
                                                           0,
                                                           G_MAXINT,
                                                           0,
                                                           G_PARAM_READWRITE | G_PARAM_CONSTRUCT));
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unvalidated monitor index (and incomplete screen coverage)
findings.invariant: Before using 'monitor' to index the Xinerama/RandR monitor geometry table, the implementation must clamp it to the live monitor count (gdk_screen_get_n_monitors), rejecting/adjusting stale or out-of-range indices.
findings.failure_mode: A monitor index set to a value >= the current monitor count (e.g. a display hot-unplugged after the property was set) indexes past the geometry array — an out-of-bounds read yielding garbage geometry, so the lock window is mis-sized/placed and may leave part of a screen uncovered.
findings.what_to_confirm: Find where the monitor field is used for geometry lookup and confirm it is clamped/validated against the current monitor count before indexing.

findings.title: lock-enabled is writable and could drop the lock if flipped mid-lifetime
findings.code:
PROP_LOCK_ENABLED,
                                         g_param_spec_boolean ("lock-enabled",
                                                               NULL,
                                                               NULL,
                                                               FALSE,
                                                               G_PARAM_READWRITE));
findings.bug_class: Policy bypass via mutable lock-state flag (improper access control)
findings.invariant: lock-enabled must be latched when the window is displayed and must not be re-evaluated in a way that lets a live, shown window turn from locking to merely blanking without re-authentication.
findings.failure_mode: A stale or racing property write sets lock-enabled to FALSE on a displayed window; a later dismiss then emits 'deactivated' and unblanks without ever prompting for authentication.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check gs_window_set_property and the deactivate path: confirm lock-enabled is read at lock time and cannot be toggled to bypass the auth prompt on an active lock.

audit_checklist: gs_window_real_grab_broken: on GdkEventGrabBroken it must always re-acquire the keyboard/pointer grab (with retry) or fail closed — never continue shown with no grab.
gs_window_real_key_press_event: no key path may unlock, spawn, mnemonic-activate, or focus a background window without successful authentication.
logout-command and keyboard-command spawn sites: argv-style g_spawn (no shell parsing) and values sourced only from trusted root/policy configuration.
Embedded on-screen-keyboard helper launched from keyboard-command must be sandboxed and unable to launch programs or open file dialogs while locked.
gs_window_real_popup_menu: unconditionally suppress context menus for the window and every child widget.
gs_window_real_visibility_notify_event: re-raise and re-assert grab/focus on any obscure event; keep the 'obscured' property consistent.
Monitor index from PROP_MONITOR must be clamped to gdk_screen_get_n_monitors() before indexing geometry arrays, and re-validated on monitor hot-plug/unplug.
lock-enabled must be latched at display time so it cannot be flipped to drop the lock without re-authentication.
gs_window_finalize must release the grab and any spawned helper/child so unlock does not leave dangling input state.
